session.title: tessel storage-layer extraction — refactor, flake, CI drift, release

compaction_events.note: Auto-compaction fired at ~148k tokens while the CI diagnosis was concluding (transcript line ~610). Everything after this point was reduced against the compaction summary, not raw pre-compaction context. Nodes created or updated after the seam carry provenance into 'c-mega-0630.compact-1.summary' where they assert things about pre-compaction events. NOTE: 'after_node' names the chronologically-last node touched before the seam — but this map is consolidated, not chronological, so spine-earlier nodes (n05, n06 under the refactor topic) are chronologically POST-seam. See mock-c.md, design questions.

nodes.title: Extract storage backend from tessel engine
nodes.summary: Split the monolithic tessel/store.py (direct sqlite3 calls) into a tessel/storage/ package: a five-method StorageBackend ABC, SqliteBackend as the default, a new JsonBackend, and a deprecation shim keeping the old import path alive. Completed after two forced detours (a test flake, then a CI matrix drift) that the extraction itself surfaced.

history.summary: Extract SqliteStore out of engine.py so a second backend becomes possible.
history.superseded_because: Scope grew on first contact: the engine touched sqlite3 in 14 call sites, so the extraction needed a real interface, not a class move.

history.summary: StorageBackend interface extracted and SqliteBackend carved out; completion blocked — the reorganized test suite surfaced an intermittent failure that has to be understood first.
history.superseded_because: Refactor resumed and completed after the flake and a CI matrix drift were resolved; JsonBackend and the compat shim landed post-compaction.

nodes.title: Map engine ↔ sqlite coupling
nodes.summary: Audited engine.py: 14 call sites reach into sqlite3 directly (cursor handling in 9, connection lifecycle in 3, PRAGMA tuning in 2). Grouped them into five capability clusters — read, write, delete, scan, flush — which became the interface surface.

nodes.title: StorageBackend = five-method ABC, sqlite stays default
nodes.summary: The interface is an ABC with exactly the five methods the engine actually uses; no speculative surface. SqliteBackend remains the default so existing configs are untouched.

quote.text: make it an ABC with exactly the five calls the engine actually uses — read, write, delete, scan, flush. sqlite stays the default, nobody's config should change.

nodes.title: Carve out SqliteBackend behind the interface
nodes.summary: Moved sqlite logic into tessel/storage/sqlite_backend.py behind the ABC; engine.py now depends only on StorageBackend. Suite green on first run — but a second full run failed test_concurrent_write intermittently, which opened the flake investigation.

residuals.label: Premature green
residuals.detail: First full-suite run passed and the extraction was declared complete. The pass was one lucky test-collection ordering; the second run exposed the order-dependent failure.

history.summary: SqliteBackend extracted; full suite green, extraction complete.
history.superseded_because: Second full run surfaced an intermittent test_concurrent_write failure — the green run was one lucky ordering, not a verified pass.

nodes.title: Add JsonBackend + tessel.store compat shim
nodes.summary: Implemented JsonBackend (one file per key, atomic writes via temp-file rename) passing the shared backend contract tests. tessel/store.py became a shim re-exporting from tessel.storage with a DeprecationWarning, so no downstream import breaks. [Created post-compaction; provenance is raw post-seam transcript only.]

nodes.title: Deprecation policy: old import warns until 0.9
nodes.summary: tessel.store keeps working with a DeprecationWarning for two minor versions and is removed in 0.9. No hard break outside a minor release.

quote.text: keep the old import working for two minor versions with a warning, kill it in 0.9 — I don't want a hard break in a patch release.

nodes.title: test_concurrent_write flake
nodes.summary: Intermittent failure surfaced by the refactor's test reorganization — but pre-existing: test_concurrent_write silently depended on state left in a module-scoped tmpdir by test_bulk_import. Moving tests into tests/storage/ changed collection order and unmasked it. Fixed by scoping the fixture per-test; 50/50 clean local runs.

history.summary: The new storage backend appears to have introduced a concurrency bug — test_concurrent_write fails intermittently since the extraction.
history.superseded_because: Root cause predates the refactor: order-dependent fixture state, reproducible on the pre-refactor commit. The refactor only changed collection order and unmasked it.

nodes.title: Reproduce: fails 3/10, only after test_bulk_import
nodes.summary: Ten full-suite runs: 3 failures, all in orderings where test_bulk_import ran first in the same module scope. Running test_concurrent_write alone: 20/20 green. Established the failure is order-dependent, not load-dependent.

nodes.title: Root cause: shared module-scoped tmpdir
nodes.summary: The db_dir fixture is module-scoped; test_bulk_import leaves 5k rows in the shared directory, and test_concurrent_write's row-count assertion assumes an empty store. The refactor's move of tests into tests/storage/ changed collection order, so the polluting test now sometimes runs first. Two dead-end hypotheses folded below.

residuals.label: Dead end: SQLite WAL race
residuals.detail: Hypothesis that WAL-mode journal checkpointing raced the concurrent writers. Disproved: failure reproduces with a single writer thread and journal_mode=DELETE.

residuals.label: Dead end: new backend introduced it
residuals.detail: Hypothesis that the freshly extracted SqliteBackend changed transaction boundaries. Disproved: same failure reproduces on the pre-refactor commit under the same test ordering.

nodes.title: Fix: per-test tmp_path, no shared state
nodes.summary: Replaced the module-scoped db_dir fixture with pytest's function-scoped tmp_path and made the bulk-import test clean up after itself. 50 consecutive local full-suite runs green.

nodes.title: Other order-dependent tests hiding?
nodes.summary: The same masking pattern (module-scoped fixtures + lucky ordering) could hide other order-dependent tests. A pytest-randomly sweep across the suite was proposed but deferred — never run, never scheduled. Left open at session end.

nodes.title: CI workflow drift (Python matrix)
nodes.summary: CI stayed red after the flake fix — for an unrelated, refactor-triggered reason: the workflow matrix still tests Python 3.9 while pyproject.toml has declared requires-python >=3.10 for months, and the new ABC's typing.Self annotations fail at import on 3.9. Matrix realigned to 3.10–3.12; CI green, twice.

history.summary: CI still failing after the flake fix — presumed the fix is incomplete in the CI environment.
history.superseded_because: The CI failure is independent of the flake: an import-time error on Python 3.9 introduced by the refactor's typing.Self usage, exposing a matrix/pyproject drift.

nodes.title: Diagnose: typing.Self breaks 3.9 at import
nodes.summary: CI logs show ImportError on Python 3.9 only — the new StorageBackend uses typing.Self (3.11+; backported acceptably to 3.10 via typing_extensions already in deps, but 3.9 is genuinely unsupported). pyproject.toml has required >=3.10 since 0.6; the workflow matrix was never updated. The drift, not the flake fix, is what keeps CI red. [Chronologically the last node touched before compaction.]

nodes.title: Drop 3.9 from matrix, add 3.12
nodes.summary: Align the CI matrix with the package's declared support: remove 3.9, keep 3.10–3.11, add 3.12. No code contortions to keep an unsupported version importable. [Created post-compaction; quote anchors to raw post-seam lines.]

quote.text: drop 3.9 from the matrix, we declared 3.10 in pyproject months ago — CI should follow the package, not the other way around.

nodes.title: Verify: CI green on 3.10–3.12, twice
nodes.summary: Updated the workflow matrix, pushed, and ran the pipeline twice back-to-back to confirm both the matrix fix and that the flake fix holds in CI's ordering. Both runs fully green across all three Python versions.

nodes.title: Release prep: tessel 0.8.0
nodes.summary: With the refactor complete and CI trustworthy again, prepared the release: version decision, changelog covering the backend interface and the deprecation policy, and a local build check. Publish itself deferred to Pi.

nodes.title: Ship as 0.8.0, not 0.7.4
nodes.summary: A new public backend interface is a feature: minor bump to 0.8.0, not a patch. Also sets the clock for the 0.9 shim removal decided under the refactor topic.

quote.text: call it 0.8.0 — a new public backend interface is a feature, not a patch.

nodes.title: Changelog + local build check
nodes.summary: Wrote the 0.8.0 changelog (backend ABC, JsonBackend, deprecation window, CI matrix change), bumped the version, and verified sdist/wheel build cleanly in a local dry run. Publishing left for Pi to trigger.
